stories.headline: EQT acquires Coller Capital in undisclosed deal adding nearly $50bn of private markets assets under management
stories.summary: EQT, the Stockholm-listed global investment organisation, has announced the acquisition of Coller Capital, a leading global secondaries firm managing nearly $50bn of assets across private equity and private credit secondaries. The deal value was not disclosed. Coller Capital provides liquidity solutions to both general partners and limited partners and operates across 11 global offices with 330 professionals. The acquisition materially strengthens EQT's position in the private markets secondaries space — a segment experiencing structural growth as LP portfolios mature and demand for portfolio liquidity increases. EQT was advised by MarshBerry on the transaction; Coller Capital's legal advisers were not publicly disclosed at announcement. Completion is conditional on regulatory approvals across multiple jurisdictions. The deal represents one of the largest consolidations in the private markets industry in recent years, adding a recognised brand in the secondaries market to EQT's existing private equity, real assets, and credit platform.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer has the deepest advisory relationship with EQT in Europe and would be the natural lead for the corporate M&A structuring and any UK regulatory filings. Clifford Chance's financial regulation practice would advise on FCA authorisation implications for Coller's UK-regulated entities, given that a change of control of an FCA-authorised firm requires formal FCA approval under FSMA 2000 — a process that runs in parallel with any CMA or competition filings. Linklaters' investment management practice is also a likely candidate given their strong LP and fund governance advisory work in the secondaries market.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Kirkland & Ellis is the dominant adviser for EQT's fund formations globally and would hold the fund-level mandate on any restructuring of EQT's fund terms post-acquisition. Debevoise & Plimpton has the deepest institutional expertise in the secondaries market specifically — they have advised on more GP-led secondaries transactions than any other firm — making them the likely lead for Coller's fund documentation review. Weil Gotshal & Manges is also active in PE consolidation mandates of this type.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Freshfields, a first-seat trainee would be coordinating due diligence on Coller's LP base, checking key-person provisions and transfer restrictions in fund documentation, and tracking regulatory filings across the UK, EU, and US simultaneously. At Kirkland, a junior associate would be reviewing EQT's existing fund governing documents for change-of-control consent requirements and drafting LP notification letters required under each fund's limited partnership agreement. The global secondaries market reached $130bn in transaction volume in 2025, driven by a 40% increase in GP-led continuation vehicle transactions.

stories.talkingPoint: The EQT-Coller Capital deal reflects how the largest PE managers are building vertically integrated private markets platforms — and why firms with integrated fund formation, M&A, and financial regulation capabilities are capturing the advisory work that pure-play corporate teams cannot handle alone.

stories.headline: MFG and Barrenjoey propose merger to create diversified Australian financial services platform with London advisory implications
stories.summary: Magellan Financial Group and Barrenjoey, the boutique investment bank backed by Barclays and international institutional investors, have proposed a merger to create a leading diversified financial services firm in Australia. The deal is structured as a merger of equals and is subject to regulatory approvals from ASIC and FIRB. The transaction has London relevance given Barrenjoey's Barclays connection and the involvement of several UK institutional investors as anchor shareholders. The combined entity would have approximately A$100bn of funds under management and advice. No legal advisers were disclosed at announcement.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Herbert Smith Freehills, with its pre-eminent Australian practice and strong London-Sydney corridor capability, is the natural lead for the UK-side corporate structuring and any FCA implications arising from Barclays' stake restructuring. Linklaters' financial institutions group would advise on the UK regulatory treatment of Barclays' reduced stake post-merger and any consequential changes to Barclays' own regulatory capital calculations. Allen & Overy Shearman's Australian practice — the largest of any Magic Circle firm in the region — would position them strongly for the local-side work.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Sullivan & Cromwell and Davis Polk, both of which advise the major institutional investors with exposure to Barrenjoey's capital structure, would be engaged to advise on the investor consent process and any Rule 144A implications if the combined entity pursues US institutional capital. The cross-border structuring work — particularly managing the US securities law overlay on the Australian regulatory process — sits firmly in their wheelhouse.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Herbert Smith Freehills, a trainee in the financial institutions team would be mapping Barclays' existing regulatory consent obligations under the FSMA 2000 change-of-control regime and coordinating with the Australian team on FIRB filing requirements. At Sullivan & Cromwell, a junior associate would be reviewing the shareholder agreements for US investors and advising on their consent rights under the proposed merger terms. Australian M&A volumes reached a five-year high in 2025, driven by financial services consolidation and inbound cross-border acquisition activity from Asian and European acquirers.

stories.talkingPoint: The MFG-Barrenjoey merger illustrates how boutique investment banks backed by major institutional investors are consolidating into integrated platforms — and why the London-Sydney regulatory corridor is increasingly a specialisation in its own right for Magic Circle firms with strong antipodean practices.

stories.headline: Doug Gurr confirmed as CMA Chair, signalling continued focus on digital markets and tech sector enforcement
stories.summary: The UK government has confirmed Doug Gurr — former President of Amazon UK and a government adviser during the Covid-19 pandemic — as the new Chair of the Competition and Markets Authority, replacing Lord Tyrie. The appointment is subject to parliamentary approval. Gurr's background in digital commerce and logistics is widely interpreted as a signal that the CMA will maintain its aggressive stance on digital markets, platform economy regulation, and the Strategic Market Status regime introduced under the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024. The CMA has several pending Phase 2 investigations and market studies in the technology sector, including its ongoing cloud computing review.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Linklaters and Clifford Chance, which have the two deepest CMA advisory practices in the market, will be immediately briefing clients on what Gurr's appointment signals for pending and anticipated CMA interventions — particularly in digital markets, cloud infrastructure, and AI. Herbert Smith Freehills' competition team is consistently instructed by major tech and media companies facing CMA scrutiny and will be updating risk assessments for clients with ongoing Phase 1 and Phase 2 matters. Freshfields' competition practice, which advised on several of the largest tech mergers reviewed by the CMA in recent years, is equally well-positioned given its strong US tech firm relationships.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Cleary Gottlieb and Gibson Dunn — both with established London competition practices — will be advising their US tech, PE, and pharmaceutical clients on how Gurr's appointment affects their CMA risk calculus on pending transactions and market investigations. Latham & Watkins' EU/UK competition practice is particularly active in advising on the new DMCC Act's Strategic Market Status regime, which is where Gurr's Amazon experience is most directly relevant.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Linklaters, a trainee in the competition team would be preparing a client briefing note on Gurr's prior positions, his stated policy views on digital markets, and the implications for the CMA's pending investigations, drawing on Hansard records and parliamentary committee testimony. At Cleary Gottlieb, a junior associate would be updating a risk matrix for US clients with UK competition exposure, flagging which pending transactions are most likely to attract Phase 2 scrutiny under Gurr's leadership. The CMA received 57 merger filings in 2025, up 18% on the prior year, with digital and AI-adjacent transactions representing the fastest-growing category.

stories.talkingPoint: Gurr's appointment as CMA Chair is a direct signal that UK competition enforcement will remain data-driven and commercially literate — but his Amazon background also means he understands exactly how digital platforms are structured, which makes him a more formidable counterparty than regulators who only know markets from the outside.

stories.headline: AI data centre energy demand drives record electricity cost pressure on UK and European grid operators
stories.summary: New analysis from energy market researchers confirms that AI data centres are materially driving up electricity costs across UK and European grids, with some regional markets recording electricity rate increases of 15–30% attributable to large-scale AI infrastructure demand. The surge is driven by the energy intensity of training large language models and the advanced cooling systems required. UK grid operator National Grid has confirmed that data centre connection requests have tripled since 2023, creating a queue of applicants seeking grid connection agreements. The UK government is consulting on a fast-track grid connection regime for strategically important data infrastructure.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Herbert Smith Freehills' energy and infrastructure practice is best positioned to advise data centre developers on grid connection agreements, planning consents, and long-term power purchase agreements under the new fast-track regime. Linklaters and Clifford Chance are both active in advising power purchase agreement counterparties — including the renewable energy developers supplying the data centres — on the commercial and regulatory terms of long-term offtake contracts. Allen & Overy Shearman's project finance team has been particularly active in financing the renewable energy infrastructure being contracted to serve AI data centre demand.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Latham & Watkins' energy regulatory practice advises the major US hyperscalers — Microsoft, Google, Amazon — on their European energy procurement strategies, including negotiating corporate PPAs and navigating UK and EU grid access regimes. Kirkland & Ellis' infrastructure team is structuring colocation and power purchase arrangements for AI data centres across the UK, working alongside Latham on the energy regulatory side to deliver the full transactional package for US tech clients.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Herbert Smith Freehills, a trainee in the energy team would be reviewing grid connection agreement terms, researching the planning permission framework for large-scale data infrastructure under the Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects regime, and drafting due diligence reports on the regulatory status of grid connection applications. At Latham, a junior associate would be negotiating the commercial terms of a long-term PPA between a UK offshore wind developer and a hyperscaler client, focusing on price indexation, volume flexibility, and force majeure provisions. UK data centre capacity is projected to double by 2028, requiring an estimated £40bn of investment in grid reinforcement and renewable generation.

stories.talkingPoint: The AI energy story is not primarily a tech story — it's a legal and regulatory story about who gets grid access, on what terms, and how the cost of that access is allocated across the system, which is why energy lawyers at the leading infrastructure practices are as busy as their M&A colleagues right now.

stories.headline: Arbitration Act 2025 enters full force, reshaping procedural dynamics for London-seated international arbitrations
stories.summary: The Arbitration Act 2025 has come fully into force, implementing the most significant reform of the UK arbitration framework since the Arbitration Act 1996. Key changes include codification of the ENRC principles on arbitrator conflicts and document production, new emergency arbitrator provisions, a default rule giving arbitrators jurisdiction to determine their own jurisdiction, and revised summary dismissal powers. The Act is intended to maintain London's competitive position as the pre-eminent global seat for international commercial arbitration. The LCIA, ICC, and CIArb have updated their procedural rules to align with the new statutory framework.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer and Herbert Smith Freehills — the two firms with the largest London international arbitration practices by caseload — will be the primary advisers updating institutional clients on how the Act changes their dispute resolution posture and contract drafting strategy. Clifford Chance's international arbitration group was actively involved in the Law Commission consultation that led to the Act and has already begun issuing detailed client guidance on the procedural changes. Slaughter and May's disputes practice is focused particularly on how the new summary dismissal provisions will affect financial services and corporate disputes, where early strike-out applications are frequently deployed.
whyItMatters.usFirms: Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan and Gibson Dunn are the US firms most active in London-seated LCIA and ICC arbitrations and are updating their US and international institutional clients on how the Act's changes affect procedural strategy in currently pending proceedings. WilmerHale's London international arbitration group has been particularly active in advising on the new arbitrator appointment and challenge provisions, which codify and in some respects expand on the existing common law framework.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: At Freshfields, a trainee in the international arbitration team would be reviewing existing arbitration clauses in major client contracts to identify those referencing the 1996 Act by name or version, and preparing updated standard clauses for client adoption. At Quinn Emanuel, a junior associate would be advising on whether transitional provisions of the new Act apply to a currently pending LCIA arbitration and mapping out the strategic implications of the new summary dismissal regime for the merits of the client's case. London handled over $23bn of disputed value in LCIA arbitrations in 2025, a record that underscores the continuing centrality of English law and English-seated arbitration to global commercial dispute resolution.

stories.talkingPoint: The Arbitration Act 2025 is the UK's clearest statement since Brexit that English law and London arbitration will compete aggressively for the global dispute resolution market — and the codification of the ENRC principles on document production removes one of the few procedural disadvantages that London-seated arbitration had versus Swiss and Singapore seats.

sectorWatch: Private equity dealmaking is showing its clearest signs of structural recovery since rates peaked: LP distributions have improved, credit markets have reopened at workable margins, and the bid-ask gap that paralysed M&A in 2023–2024 is compressing. The watch this week is whether improved sentiment converts into completed transactions — the pipeline is strong but execution remains the bottleneck as complex regulatory processes (CMA, FCA, NSI Act) stretch timelines.
oneToFollow: Watch the CMA's formal investigation into cloud computing market concentration — expected to move to Phase 2 this week — which could impose structural remedies on hyperscaler providers and has direct implications for technology M&A and commercial contracts across every sector.
